food_subgroup: Prepared seafood dishes — stuffed and braised

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product class; no specific entry for stuffed braised squid exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers prepared squid products; no specific code for stuffed squid with braised sauce exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for stuffed squid dish. SNOMED CT coverage of prepared composite seafood dishes is limited.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 150 g — one to two stuffed squid tubes with sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Sotong Sumbat is one to two stuffed squid tubes with braising sauce, weighing approximately 150 g, as served in a Malaysian or Singaporean home meal or mixed-rice stall.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Squid, mixed species, cooked (SR Legacy ID 15150) and Coconut milk, raw (SR Legacy ID 12117); composite estimation for stuffed braised squid dish
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Sotong Sumbat exists in USDA FoodData Central, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or HPB Singapore Foodo as of the access date. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked squid (FDC ID 175168) as the primary protein base, supplemented with USDA coconut milk data for fat and energy contributions from the masak lemak sauce, and USDA egg data for the filling component. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) were cross-checked against this composite and retained where consistent. Saturated fat estimated at 3.8 g/100 g based on coconut milk contribution proportional to sauce fraction (~20% of dish weight). Cholesterol estimated at 185 mg/100 g based on squid (~260 mg/100 g cooked) and egg filling contribution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on high-protein, high-fat seafood dish with minimal carbohydrate from sauce
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sotong Sumbat. GI estimated at 35 (low) by analogy with high-protein, high-fat seafood dishes with small carbohydrate contributions from sauce aromatics and coconut milk. The dominant macronutrients (protein 13.5 g, fat 9.2 g per 100 g) substantially blunt postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (35 × 6.75) / 100 = 2.36, rounded to 2.4. This is consistent with the low-GL category (<10).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 310 mg per 100 g; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 465 mg sodium, representing ~20% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by major cardiovascular guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Sotong Sumbat to occasional consumption, request reduced-sauce portions, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes in the same meal to keep total sodium within the daily target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load per serving (~2.4), with high protein (13.5 g/100 g) and moderate fat that collectively blunt postprandial glucose excursion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sotong Sumbat is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions (150 g), as the low GI and high protein content support glycaemic control; however, coconut milk-based sauce variants add saturated fat and calories that should be accounted for in the overall meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Squid is naturally high in cholesterol (~185 mg/100 g estimated) and the coconut milk sauce contributes saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g); however, squid is low in total fat relative to red meat and provides beneficial selenium and vitamin B12.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Sotong Sumbat in moderation, preferring sambal-based sauce variants over masak lemak (coconut milk) preparations to reduce saturated fat intake, and should monitor overall dietary cholesterol from all sources across the day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Squid provides selenium (35 mcg/100 g) and high-quality protein; aromatics such as turmeric and ginger have documented anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sotong Sumbat may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; those sensitive to saturated fat should prefer sambal-based preparations over coconut milk variants to minimise pro-inflammatory fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sotong Sumbat is a traditional Malay dish consisting of whole squid tubes (sotong) that are stuffed with a savoury filling and then braised in a flavourful sauce. The filling typically comprises whisked eggs — which steam into a soft, custard-like texture inside the squid — or a mixture of minced chicken or beef combined with aromatics such as shallots, garlic, and ginger. Once stuffed, the squid tubes are secured with toothpicks to prevent the filling from escaping during cooking. The dish is then braised in one of two classic sauce styles: a spicy sambal base made from blended dried chillies, shrimp paste, and aromatics, or a rich masak lemak gravy made with coconut milk and turmeric, which gives the dish a golden-yellow colour and a creamy, mildly spiced flavour. The finished dish offers a pleasing textural contrast between the tender, slightly chewy squid exterior and the soft, savoury filling. Sotong Sumbat is commonly served as a side dish in Malaysian and Singaporean home cooking and at mixed-rice (nasi campur) stalls, typically accompanied by steamed white rice. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with a low glycaemic index, making it broadly suitable for blood sugar management. It is a notable source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and niacin from the squid, and provides vitamin A and iron from the egg filling and aromatics. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on sauce preparation, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal.
common_user_queries: How is stuffed squid made in Malay cooking?
Is Sotong Sumbat suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between Sotong Sumbat sambal and masak lemak?
Is stuffed squid high in cholesterol?
Can I eat Sotong Sumbat if I have high blood pressure?
What does Sotong Sumbat taste like?
meal_context: side dish for mixed rice (nasi campur)

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Sotong Sumbat exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, USDA FoodData Central) as of the access date. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked squid, coconut milk, and egg data as component proxies. The AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9) were cross-checked against this composite and are broadly consistent. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature, not a direct measurement.
notes: 1. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite stuffed-and-braised squid dish is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source consulted as of 2026-04-16; detailed moisture data for this traditional preparation is unavailable. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4) are estimated from USDA fatty acid profiles for cooked squid and coconut milk in proportion to their estimated contribution to the dish; these are approximate and should be verified if precise fatty acid data are required. 3. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/absent in natural squid and coconut milk preparations per USDA FoodData Central. 4. cholesterol_mg (185.0) is estimated from USDA cooked squid (~260 mg/100 g) adjusted for the egg filling and sauce dilution effect; this is an approximation. 5. Saturated fat (3.8 g/100 g) is estimated based on coconut milk contributing approximately 20% of dish weight in a masak lemak preparation; sambal variants will have lower saturated fat. 6. The input source cited ('Malaysian Journal of Nutrition', nutriweb.org.my) is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence; USDA FoodData Central has been used instead as the approved secondary global source. 7. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Sotong Sumbat was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the access date.
